coredata.dc:description: Novel material innovation is a driving force for advancing high-performance electrochemical energy storage technologies. Quantum dots (QDs), over the last decade, have exhibited immense potential in applications related to bioimaging, optoelectronics, catalysis, and energy storage, together with a remarkable rise in greener synthesis methods. Carbon nanomaterials, particularly carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), have garnered significant interest due to their exceptional characteristics, including high electrical conductivity, thermal stability, mechanical robustness, chemical durability, photoluminescence, affordability, and ease of surface modification. CQDs show promise for supercapacitors due to their unique properties but face challenges like limited surface area. Improving CQD synthesis and purification is crucial for enhancing supercapacitor performance. GQDs are praised for their conductive networks and surface properties, but more research is needed on industrial-scale synthesis. This review reported the recent advances in the electrochemical characteristics and synthesis of various QDs in supercapacitor electrodes.

head.ce:title: Comparative studies of recent advances in quantum dots nanocomposites for supercapacitor electrodes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel material innovation is a driving force for advancing high-performance electrochemical energy storage technologies. Quantum dots (QDs), over the last decade, have exhibited immense potential in applications related to bioimaging, optoelectronics, catalysis, and energy storage, together with a remarkable rise in greener synthesis methods. Carbon nanomaterials, particularly carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), have garnered significant interest due to their exceptional characteristics, including high electrical conductivity, thermal stability, mechanical robustness, chemical durability, photoluminescence, affordability, and ease of surface modification. CQDs show promise for supercapacitors due to their unique properties but face challenges like limited surface area. Improving CQD synthesis and purification is crucial for enhancing supercapacitor performance. GQDs are praised for their conductive networks and surface properties, but more research is needed on industrial-scale synthesis. This review reported the recent advances in the electrochemical characteristics and synthesis of various QDs in supercapacitor electrodes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The idea of QDs or synthetic atoms emerged due to efforts to decrease the size of semiconductors. The QDs have demonstrated remarkable features as fluorescent electrolytes and supercapacitor electrode materials. The QDs have shown promise in enhancing the performance of supercapacitors through their unique properties and potential applications. The QDs have been combined with various metals and polymers to create electrodes with exceptional electrochemical properties. The QDs quantum capacitance effect facilitates the storage of extra charges through large energy density bands. Integrating supercapacitors with various electronic components is crucial for widespread future use. The characteristics of QDs, such as thickness, size, edge configuration, and defects, can be adjusted for specific applications, with slight changes in interactions leading to significant alterations in properties [1]. Quantum mechanical phenomena determine the electrical and optical characteristics of QDs. Consequently, QDs display intermediate characteristics between those of bulk substances and those of singular atoms or molecules. QDs can be synthesized from a range of materials including metal atoms, metal oxides, metal sulfides, and carbon-based materials. Their nanoscale size imparts unique attributes that enable diverse applications in various fields. Supercapacitors commonly utilize QDs as part of composites with electrochemical activity. The extensive surface area of QDs allows for efficient charge storage and high current density during discharge. Quantum-sized nanoparticles have a high density of active sites on their surface, enabling effective covalent modification. Functional groups on the surface of nanoparticles aid in forming well-dispersed QDs and interactions with other composite components. The involvement of QDs in charge transfer processes is crucial for their role in electrical energy storage. Fig. 1 illustrates the benefits of QDs-based supercapacitors as outlined in the articles available in Scopus. QDs-based supercapacitors are characterized by their exceptional stability and flexibility, which can be further classified into two categories: CQDs and GQDs.

Nanomaterials such as graphene nanoplatelets or other small carbon aggregates are called CQDs [2]. GQDs are nanostructures of zero dimensions composed of single or multilayer graphene sheets. GQDs are distinguished by their high density of active sites, extensive specific surface area (SSA), and structural edges that facilitate the processes of ion adsorption and desorption. The combination of numerous active sites, large surface areas, and excellent electrical properties make graphene sheets with GQDs suitable for supercapacitors. The presence of GQDs can significantly improve the capacitive output of supercapacitors by enhancing the interface between electrolytes and electrodes. The GQDs offer a promising avenue for the development of high-performance supercapacitors due to their unique structural and functional properties [3]. The graph in Fig. 2 illustrates the progressive increase in published articles over the past few years focusing on supercapacitors based on QDs, as sourced from Scopus.

Fullerenes and CQDs represent the oldest and newest zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, respectively. Zero-dimensional carbon materials' electronic structure and electrochemical properties make them highly valuable for developing supercapacitors. Fullerenes can accept up to six electrons in distinct steps, making them ideal for constructing electrochemical supercapacitors and batteries. GQDs and CQDs exhibit a less well-defined structure than fullerenes. Materials containing these QDs possess a high surface area, numerous chemically active sites on their surfaces and edges, rapid electron transport characteristics, and an enhanced ion diffusion rate. Incorporating GQDs or CQDs into supercapacitor electrode materials significantly boosts the performance of charge storage devices.

The effectiveness of GQDs or CQDs in fabricating materials for supercapacitors stems from the ease of modifying their surfaces, the potential for doping their structures with heteroatoms, the capability for swift electron transfer, and their extensive SSA, leading to the attainment of elevated electrochemical capacitance. Structural imperfections, which increase the number of active sites and diffusion pathways, enhance the electronic structure and electrical conductivity of carbon-based QDs. The resultant structures exhibit heightened charge-storage capacity and accelerated kinetics of metal-ion transport [4]. Fig. 3 displays the keyword analysis conducted on the latest publications sourced from Scopus, utilizing the VOSviewer software.

The analysis of keywords related to QDs-based supercapacitors, including CQDs, GQDs, semiconductor QDs, symmetric/asymmetric configurations, flexibility, nanosheets, nanorods, and nanoflowers, is presented in Fig. 3. Recently, the supercapacitors with CQDs/NiCo [5–7], GQDs/polyacrylonitrile (PAN) [8,9], Boron QDs [10], CQDs/Cu/polyaniline (PANI) [11], GQDs/PANI [12], GQDs/PPy [13], GQDs/rGO [14], vanadium nitride QDs [15], SnS2 QDs/Cu [16], Ag QDs [17,18], SnO2 QDs [19–21], NiWO4 QDs/Mn [22], RuO2 QDs [23], and CuS QDs [24] electrode materials have been developed. GQDs are characterized by a sp2 hybridized atom structure, while CQDs have an amorphous structure dominated by sp3 hybridization. GQDs possess a more regular 2-D structure and higher reactivity than CQDs, making them advantageous in various applications. Carbon-based QDs, offer good dispersibility in both water and nonaqueous solvents, along with easy covalent or non-covalent modifications, enabling their incorporation into other nanomaterial structures for the development of hierarchically organized nanostructured materials with potential applications in energy-storage devices. While much attention has been given to improving electrodes, efforts to enhance device performance through electrolyte modification have been less frequent but equally important. Adding small amounts of additives to electrolytes has proven to be an effective strategy in forming appropriate solid-electrolyte interphases and enhancing ionic conductivity. The CQDs and GQDs have emerged as promising electrolyte additives in energy storage devices due to their positive impact on performance. The unique physiochemical properties of CQDs and GQDs make them ideal candidates for use as fillers in solid-state electrolytes, further improving the overall performance of supercapacitors [25]. To address the limitations associated with electrolytes in energy storage devices, including issues like limited lifespan, flammability, and restricted voltage range, the performance can be improved by using additives, even though they are typically present in small quantities (usually less than 5 %).

Despite the existence of numerous organic and inorganic electrolyte additives in the literature, there is an ongoing need to discover novel, durable, and environmentally friendly additives for long-term use [26]. Electrolyte additives in electrochemical energy storage devices encompass a wide range of substances, among which are CQDs and GQDs. These additives are distinct from others due to their unique properties. Although the majority of additives exist as molecules, salts, or chemical compounds that are dissolved within electrolytes, CQDs possess the unique ability to dissolve in liquid media due to their numerous functional groups and extremely small size. The unique nanostructures of CQDs and GQDs have attracted considerable interest as viable substitutes for traditional metal-based semiconductor quantum dots. This shift is primarily attributed to their exceptional attributes, which include superior photoluminescence, high quantum yield, scalability, adjustable emission properties, biocompatibility, chemical stability, and excellent hydrophilicity. The distinctive features of these zero-dimensional carbon materials have inspired researchers to explore their potential in various fields, including bioimaging, optoelectronics, catalysis, and energy storage applications [27]. Baslak et al. [28] utilized hydrothermal synthesis through a green methodology to produce CQDs and showed a specific capacitance (SC) of 2.12 F/g with an efficiency of around 58 % in the initial cycle. A novel graphitic carbon nitride/carbon hybrid material was developed by carbonizing the crude product mixture obtained during the synthesis of QDs and achieved an SC of 146.67 F/g [29]. Transition metals have a significant impact on the electrochemical energy storage process due to their valence and coordination structure. A bimetallic-based electrode material, Co0.03Ni0.97 LDH, can be more active through CQDs. The CQDs facilitate fast charge transfer between Co and Ni and enhance energy density. The CQDs/Co0.03Ni0.97 LDH electrode exhibited an SC of 2408 F/g and capacitance retention (CR) of 90 % after 2000 cycles [30].

A study investigated the synthesis of lignin-derived hierarchical porous carbons integrated with CQDs. This optimization resulted in many accessible storage sites, enhancing ion migration capabilities and stability, ultimately forming a comprehensive conductive network with cycling stability of 103 % CR after 10,000 cycles [31]. Wang et al. [33] reported a hybrid material based on the CQDs/Ti3C2Tx which MXene with its high conductivity and SSA, facilitates rapid charge transfer and provides high double-layer capacitance through ion adsorption. In another study, the CQDs/CoS2 nanocomposite electrode material exhibited a high SC of 808 F/g and a CR of 98.75 % over 10,000 cycles [35]. The composite of GQDs is extensively used in supercapacitors [36]. A study reported the synthesis and characterization of niobium-doped strontium cobaltite (SCN) by the sol–gel process [37]. The SCN@GQD composite showed considerable promise as an electrode material and higher SC than GQDs and SCN. That study showed this was reasonably ascribed to better conductivity, more oxygen vacancies, and faster ion diffusion. Transition metal oxide composites combined with carbon have been known as practical materials for enhancing electrochemical properties in supercapacitor applications. Kharangarh et al. [38] prepared an electrode based on the composite structure of nickel-cobaltite (NiCo2O4)/GQDs. They achieved an SC of 481.4 F g−1 at a current density of 0.35 Ag−1. This is due to the improvement in electrical conductivity and the increase in ion diffusion rates between the electrodes and the electrolyte. A symmetric supercapacitor with GQDs/reduced graphene oxide (rGO) electrode exhibited a remarkable SC of 277.8 F/g and cycle stability with a CR rate of 92.7 % after 10,000 cycles [39]. The incorporation of crystalline GQDs into activated carbon (AC) was found to enhance both electrochemical efficiency by improving charge-transfer and ion migration kinetics and SC with excellent cycling stability [40]. Liu et al. [41] fabricated nanoflowers based on the FeCo2S4/NH2-GQDs nanocomposite and achieved an SC of 2314.8 F/g with a CR of 95.1 % after 5000 cycles. Transition metals' valence and coordination in electrode materials are very critical to the mechanism of electrochemical energy storage. One such study has been done on Co0.03Ni0.97LDH, a class of bimetallic electrode material that essentially shows very low electrochemical activity and can easily be transformed into its highly active version by using a simple electrochemical activation process with the help of CQDs [42]. The results revealed that CQDs promote charge transfer, ensuring the efficiency of the Co and Ni valence regulation and an increase of Co3+ and Ni2+ concentrations, hence enhancing energy density. In another study, a scalable film of CQDs was developed on nitrogen plasma-treated carbon cloth through an electrodeposition process and realized a 3.4-fold increase in SC of the supercapacitor [43]. Further mechanism analysis indicates that such enhancement in the capacitance of the CQD-based film is mainly due to the optimization of edge sites with the increase in graphitized sp2 carbon structures and forming a graphene-like architecture in the film. Recently, carbon nanomaterials have been extensively used in supercapacitors [44,46–48] and sensors [47–62]. This review reported the recent advances in the electrochemical characteristics and synthesis of various QDs in supercapacitor electrodes. The electrical properties of the supercapacitors such as specific capacitance (SC), energy density (ED), power density (PD), and stability have been compared for QDs-based supercapacitors.

The emergence of CQDs has attracted significant interest as a novel carbon-based material for supercapacitor applications. Renowned for their exceptional electrical conductivity and significant SSA, CQDs enable rapid charge transfer and enhance capacitance, making them a promising candidate in energy storage technologies. [63]. The presence of numerous functional groups and the incorporation of heteroatoms on their surfaces enhance both pseudocapacitance and the wettability of the electrolyte [64]. The simple synthesis process and versatility of CQDs facilitate the creation of a wide range of composites and structural designs. However, employing CQDs alone as electrode materials poses certain difficulties, including a reduced energy density when compared to alternative carbon materials such as graphene and carbon nanotubes. This limitation is primarily due to restricted pore volume and quantum size effects. Additionally, CQDs exhibit poorer cycling stability and rate performance at high current densities or voltages, which can be attributed to the aggregation and dissolution phenomena associated with these materials [65]. The electrical properties of CQDs-based supercapacitors have been compared in Table 1 . The escalating energy demands, driven by population growth and environmental degradation, necessitate innovative solutions for energy storage. Some research synthesizes carbon derived from agricultural and industrial waste to develop energy storage devices. Roang et al. [66] developed a novel composite electrode material of activated carbon (AC) derived bamboo tar and CQDs that achieved superior conductivity and improved pore size and SSA. The SC of the supercapacitor was reported to be 677.7 F/g, demonstrating a CR of 95.7 % following 10,000 cycles. The constructed supercapacitors, featuring electrodes made from recycled carbon fiber and a gel electrolyte containing garlic CQDs, including high SC (155 F/g), low internal resistance, and rapid charge/discharge capabilities, suggest a viable alternative to costly carbon nanotubes and graphene [67]. A study introduces an innovative and environmentally friendly method for the gradient synthesis of sodium-functionalized CQDs (Na + -CQDs), alongside nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CQDs (N/S-CQDs) and nitrogen and sulfur co-doped KOH-AC (N/S-KAC) derived from black liquor through dialysis, hydrothermal carbonization, and activation-carbonization techniques. The N/S-KAC, characterized by a high SSA of 2294 m2 g−1, demonstrated an SC of 253 F/g and CR of 93.91 % after 5000 cycles [68].

Manganese dioxide (MnO2) due to its diverse crystalline structure is extensively investigated as a positive electrode material for supercapacitors. A CQDs/MnO2 nanosheet electrode exhibited an SC of 334.5 F/g with CR of 90 % after 6000 cycles [69]. That study indicated the CQDs effectively enhance the presence of oxygen vacancies and promote low crystallinity within MnO2. Fig. 4 shows the electrochemical characteristics and process for creating the flexible electrode composed of CQDs and MnO2.

Inayat et al. [70] synthesized highly conductive CQDs through the hydrothermal oxidation of used tea leaves and obtained an SC of 302 F/g. A study synthesized CQDs and N-/S-doped CQDs encapsulated within graphene as positive electrodes [71]. The electrodes exhibited a distinctive battery-type behavior, attributed to the abundance of O-/N-/S-containing functional groups integrated within the graphitic structure of the CQDs-graphene composite, thereby increasing the availability of redox-active sites. That study mentioned the symmetric supercapacitor was constructed utilizing the S-doped CQDs, and exhibited ED and PD of 15.2 Wh kg−1, and 700 W kg−1, respectively. MXene aerogels are emerging as effective electrode materials for supercapacitors due to their superior conductivity and ease of processing. The supercapacitor with MXene/CQDs composite electrode has been investigated in Ref. [72]. The synthesized Ti3C2Tx–Arg-CQDs-Ser electrode demonstrates enhanced supercapacitor performance, achieving an SC of 525.0 F/g which significantly surpasses the performance of the Ti3C2Tx electrode. Porous carbons derived from biomass exhibit significant promise as electrode materials for supercapacitors; however, their amorphous nature and predominance of micropores hinder electrochemical kinetics, thereby restricting their rate capability and cycle stability. A study introduces an approach to enhance the performance of porous carbons by incorporating CQDs characterized by a high degree of graphitization into the porous carbon framework [73]. That study explained that the integration of CQDs results in composites with a well-structured porous architecture, enriched mesopore content, and moderate doping with heteroatoms on the surface. The charge and ion transfer kinetics are significantly enhanced due to the development of conductive pathways within the carbon matrix, alongside an optimized pore structure and surface doping. Fig. 5 illustrates the fabrication process of CQDs integrated with porous carbon and the electrochemical properties of symmetric supercapacitors (SSC).

In Ref. [74] the composite of nickel–cobalt double hydroxide (NiCo-LDH) and biomass-derived CQDs exhibited a 3-dimensional network of interconnected nanorods. The ASC configuration of NiCo-LDH-CQDs//AC achieved a high energy density of 46.47 Wh kg−1 and a power density of 800 W kg−1. A novel CQDs-NiCo2O4/C@Ni-Co–S electrode based on integrating CQDs-modified NiCo2O4 within its core and a Ni-Co–S nanosheet outer layer has been reported in Ref. [75]. The incorporation of CQDs effectively mitigates the clustering of NiCo2O4 nanoparticles while enhancing electronic conductivity. The CQDs-NiCo2O4/C@Ni-Co–S electrode showed a remarkable SC of 2396 F/g with CR of 92 % after 10,000 cycles. The recent advancements in nanomaterials have significantly propelled the growth of wearable micro-electronic technologies, characterized by their adaptability and compact design. The emergence of flexible electronics has transformed numerous fields, including robotic sensory applications, health-monitoring apparel, and adaptable display technologies. The performance of wearable devices heavily relies on micropower supplies, which must exhibit high energy density, flexibility, and safety features. Conductive polymers such as PANI, PPy, and polythiophene (PTh) are extensively researched for their high capacitance and economic viability in supercapacitor applications. A novel method involves utilizing cotton fabric as a flexible substrate for the deposition of conductive materials, exemplified by the growth of pyrrole on cotton, subsequently in a robust CC@CQD@PPy electrode with exceptional capacitance and stability, thus enhancing the potential of smart textiles and wearable technology [76]. The electrochemical characteristics of the CC@CQD@PPy electrode demonstrated an SC of 537.9 F/g. 1. Fig. 6 illustrates the FESEM analysis, along with the electrical properties and electrode fabrication processes associated with the CC@CQD@PPy supercapacitor. That study showed the electrochemical characteristics of the CC@CQD@PPy supercapacitor significantly surpassing that of numerous textile-based supercapacitors.

The exploration of biologically derived materials for the fabrication of supercapacitors is gaining traction, focusing on creating high-performance and environmentally friendly alternatives. Various biological materials have been employed in the synthesis of CQDs, where specific functional groups are crucial in enhancing the performance of supercapacitors by improving electrolyte ion adsorption and overall electrode efficiency. In a study, turmeric leaves used to extract CQDs abundant in functional groups such as phenols, carboxyl, and hydroxyl, facilitating rapid electron transfer and superior supercapacitor performance have been investigated [77]. That study achieved an SC of 468 F/g and CR of approximately 80 % after 5000 cycles for CQDs supercapacitor. The integration of CQDs as conductive dopants through a hydrothermal synthesis has demonstrated notable enhancements in both capacitance and cycle stability. Sun et al. [78] presented the synthesis of self-supported, binder-free α-Ni(OH)2 nanosheet arrays enhanced with CQDs. They found that the hierarchical architecture of α-Ni(OH)2, combined with the presence of CQDs, contributes to increased reactive sites and enhanced electrical conductivity. Also, They showed the ASC based on the CQDs/α-Ni(OH)2//AC electrodes achieved an ED of 44.0 Wh kg−1 at 3000 W kg−1. The images presented in Fig. 7 illustrate the SEM and TEM analyses of NiOH-C, alongside the electrical characteristics of the CQDs/α-Ni(OH)2//AC supercapacitor. Layered double hydroxides (LDHs), particularly NiCo-LDHs, are emerging as effective battery-type electrodes for flexible SCs, attributed to their significant SC and redox activity. However, challenges such as low conductivity and volume fluctuations remain prevalent. The development of vertical-standing nanoarray structures, exemplified by using ZIF-67 as a template for NiCo-LDHs on flexible substrates, offers a solution to these challenges. By integrating CQDs into NiCo-LDHs arrays, improvements in rate capability and stability can be achieved, leading to the creation of high-performance electrodes suitable for wearable electronics and energy storage applications [79].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are promising candidates for supercapacitor applications due to their intricate structures and extensive surface areas; however, their practical implementation is hindered by inadequate electronic conductivity. To address the conductivity challenges associated with MOFs, researchers are investigating the development of new MOFs with enhanced conductivity and the incorporation of conductive materials, such as carbon nanotubes, to improve overall performance. The integration of CQDs with NiMOF on nickel foam has been reported in Ref. [80]. That study mentioned that CQDs significantly improve the electrical conductivity of NiMOF while also modifying its morphology to reveal numerous active sites, thereby enhancing electrochemical performance. The supercapacitors based on CQDs/MOF (+)//AC (−) electrodes demonstrated a broad voltage window of 1.7 V and an ED of 67.1 Wh kg−1 at 5100 W kg−1. Fig. 8 illustrates the SEM images of the pristine MOF and CQDs integrated with the MOF, the process involved in the synthesis of the CQD/MOF composite, and the electrical characteristics of the CQD/MOF//AC supercapacitor. Mohanty et al. [81] synthesized nitrogen-doped CQDs and hematite dots (HtDs) integrated with reduced graphene (RG) (RG@CQDs/HtDs). They found the supercapacitor with RG@CDs/HtDs (+)//Mn3O4/C (−) structure achieved a potential window of 2.5 V, an ED of 134 Wh kg−1. Another study synthesized a three-dimensional interconnected architecture using graphitic carbon nitride QDs/graphene hydrogel (CNQ/GH) nanocomposites [82]. That study demonstrated the SSC with CNQ/GH electrodes exhibited an ED of 22.5 Wh kg−1 at 250 W kg−1. Also, the supercapacitors based on CQDs/MoS2 [83] and CQDs/Co3O4 [84] nanocomposites showed an advanced electrode material for future energy storage technologies.

A comparison of different types of supercapacitors based on the GQDs electrode composites has been reported in Table 2 . The increasing integration of flexible electronic technology has led to a heightened demand for adaptable energy storage solutions, primarily in the form of flexible supercapacitors. Among these, flexible-fiber supercapacitors are gaining prominence due to their superior power density, rapid charge–discharge capabilities, and inherent flexibility, allowing braiding. Carbon-based substances, such as fibers, nanotubes, and graphene, are commonly utilized as electrode materials in flexible supercapacitors. The carbon fibers exhibit exceptional mechanical strength, conductivity, and stability; however, their dense structure can hinder their electrochemical performance as electrode materials. A flexible supercapacitor based on a composite of flexible carbon fibers and GQDs has been investigated in Ref. [85]. That study achieved an SC of 27.83 F/g with PD of 7.5 W kg−1, attributed to a textured surface and GQDs. Carbon black is a widely utilized conductive agent; however, its zero-dimensional nature limits charge transfer efficiency across conductive pathways. Integrating carbon black with other carbon-based materials may address this limitation effectively. A study showed that the combination of carbon black with GQDs improved capacitance and achieved an ED of 10.54 Wh kg−1 [86]. Research has focused on various structured carbon materials, including biochar, which exhibit remarkable performance in electric double-layer capacitance. Recent studies have identified rare-earth elements such as La, Ce, and Nd as effective dopants that can enhance the electrochemical characteristics of supercapacitors. The innovative combination of erbium (Er) with GQDs in biochar-based materials represents a novel strategy to improve the performance of supercapacitors, with promising results in terms of ion transport efficiency and energy density. A novel nanocomposite comprising Er/GQDs integrated into highly porous biochar derived from coffee grounds has been introduced in Ref. [87]. Incorporating Er-GQDs nanoparticles into the biochar matrix increased the SSA and thermal stability, optimizing the material's properties and enhancing the double of SC. Fig. 9 illustrates the electrical characteristics and process of creating the Er/GQDs/biochar composite, accompanied by TEM images depicting biochar and the Er/GQDs/biochar composite.

The layered double hydroxides (LDHs), due to their extensive SSA and numerous active sites, are increasingly recognized for applications in supercapacitors. They encounter obstacles such as inadequate conductivity and stability challenges during redox reactions. Recent innovations focus on integrating carbon-based QDs with LDHs to form porous architectures. Porous LDHs synthesized from MOFs demonstrate enhanced ion flow efficiency and lower transport resistance. In a study, GQDs were integrated with nickel gallium (NiGa)-LDH, which is synthesized from Ni-MOF on nickel foam (NF), to fabricate a supercapacitor with NiGa-LDH/N-GQDs/NF electrode and an SC of 2160 F/g [88].

The realization of MXene's full capabilities is hindered by the self-weight stacking of its layered architecture, which limits the surface area accessible to ions in electrolytes. Various strategies have been investigated to improve the energy storage performance of MXene, including the intercalation of conductive polymers and carbon-based materials, with notable capacitance values achieved through incorporating PANI, PPy, and PEDOT. Feng et al. [89] combined the exfoliated MXene with GQDs and achieved an SC of 343 F/g, and 100 % CR after 10,000 cycles. In a study, a supercapacitor based on GQDs electrodeposited onto nickel foil using polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) as a binder obtained an ED of 17.78 Wh kg−1 [90]. In another study, the supercapacitor with hetero-hybrid composed of strontium titanate, GQDs, and PANI exhibited an ED of 16.2 Wh kg−1 at 4533.7 W kg−1 [91]. Incorporating sulfur and nitrogen into GQDs/PANI significantly enhanced electrical conductivity while augmenting the number of reactive sites and improved ion storage capabilities [92]. Integrating carbon materials with transition metal hydroxides in composite materials presents notable benefits. Nickel-cobalt LDHs (NiCo-LDH) demonstrate remarkable redox activity. However, their limited conductivity poses challenges that can be mitigated by incorporating conductive carbon materials such as GQDs. Yan et al. [93] developed an electrode material based on GQDs/NiCo- LDH composite. The synergistic interactions between NiCo-LDH and GQDs significantly improved the charge transfer and storage capabilities. They mentioned the supercapacitor with GQDs/NiCo-LDH electrode achieved an SC of 1671.27 F/g. Fig. 10 shows the electrochemical behavior of the GQDs/NiCo-LDH-based supercapacitor. The pseudocapacitive behavior can be ascribed to the reactions involving Ni and Co ions [93]:

A significant challenge in the conventional preparation of NiCo-LDH nanosheets is the difficulty in controlling their structure and preventing agglomeration, which can diminish the number of active sites available. Another study introduced a novel hollow nanocage configuration of NiCo-LDH nanosheets integrated with GQDs, utilizing a zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF) as a self-sacrificial template [94]. The design of the hollow nanocage effectively reduces the clustering of nanosheets and expands the electrochemical interface area, in contrast, the integration of GQDs improves electrical conductivity and offers supplementary active sites for chemical reactions. That study found the GQD/H-NiCo-LDH//AC supercapacitor achieved an SC of 2347 F/g and an ED of 52.1 Wh kg−1 at 770 W kg−1. Fig. 11 demonstrates the process involved in the creation of the GQD/H-NiCo-LDH (+) electrode and the energy levels prior to contact, the establishment of the heterojunction, and the electrical characteristics associated with the asymmetric supercapacitor. Also, a study presented a novel configuration of NiCo-LDH hollow micro-tunnels, which are intricately combined with GQDs characterized by a higher Fermi level and exhibited an SC of 1628 F/g [95]. The distinctive hollow architecture enhances the accessibility of the electrolyte to a greater number of electroactive sites, at the same time, the two-dimensional nanosheets exhibit superior surface chemistry that facilitates rapid ion and electron transfer. Jiang et al. [96] introduced a 3D self-supporting flexible architecture achieved through the in-situ growth of NiCo2O4 (NCO) nanowires, induced by graphene oxide QDs (GOQDs), which are anchored onto a 3D holey graphene (3DHG) framework. Pseudocapacitors, such as NCO, demonstrate superior capacitance but face challenges related to conductivity and capacity degradation over time. To overcome these limitations, a three-dimensional graphene skeleton (3DG) is employed to improve energy storage capabilities. They described the combination of 3DG with NCO nanowires in a honeycomb-like configuration established a robust framework for ion transport and the integration of GOQDs enhanced charge mobility and inhibited material aggregation.

Niobium tungsten oxide is recognized for its significant ion diffusion coefficient, various redox pairs, robust structural stability, and inherently low electrical conductivity. A composite of W3Nb14O44/biomass carbon and boron-doped GQDs as a supercapacitor electrode with SC of 535 F/g has been stated in Ref. [97]. The limited intrinsic conductivity and inadequate electroactive sites restrict the broader utilization of tantalum oxide in supercapacitor applications. Ref. [98] presented an electrode based on a composite of Ta1.1O1.05/biomass carbon with boron-doped GQDs. That study indicated the incorporation of boron-doped GQDs leads to the development of Ta1.1O1.05 nanorods significantly enhancing intrinsic conductivity, electroactive sites, and voltage window range. Vanadium oxide has emerged as a viable electrode material for supercapacitors due to its ability to exist in multiple oxidation states. In another research fabrication of an electrode based on a V2O5/biomass carbon nanocomposite and boron-doped GQDs has been investigated [99]. The SSC achieved an SC of 435.53 F/g with 98.9 % CR after 10,000 cycles. MnO2 stands out as a viable pseudocapacitive material with environmental sustainability, and high specific capacitance. To address the limitations associated with pure MnO2 electrodes, researchers are integrating MnO2 with carbon-based materials like GQDs to boost performance. The interaction between GQDs and MnO2 significantly improves specific capacitance and stability. A study reported a GQDs@MnO2 electrode with SC of 246 F/g and GQDs@MnO2//AC supercapacitor with ED of 29.9 Wh kg−1 at 538.0 W kg−1 [100]. A study presented a method for synthesizing Nb2O5 − graphene heterojunction composites utilizing ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid and boron-functionalized GQDs [101]. The Nb2O5/GQDs-G composite significantly enhanced intrinsic conductivity and the number of electrochemically active sites. Research showed the supercapacitor with bismuth ferrite/graphitic carbon nitride/N-doped GQDs electrode achieved an SC of 1472 F/g and an ED of 53.1 Wh kg−1 at 705.4 W kg−1 [102]. The performance of a supercapacitor was enhanced by anchoring Sn/SnO2 nanodots within carbon nanofibers and by the incorporation of GQDs [103]. GQDs enhance the surface area for electrochemical reactions, improve conductivity, and minimize interfacial resistance [104].

Table 3 presents a comparative analysis of various supercapacitor types that utilize QDs as components in their electrode composites. The successful creation of a prototype supercapacitor hinges on the design of a stable electrode, with inorganic sulfide-based transition metals being favored due to their abundant oxidative states and cost-effectiveness. Ref. [105] described a tungsten disulfide (WS2) QDs-enhanced WO3 electrode for supercapacitor applications. The WS2/WO3//AC ASC achieved an ED and PD of 55.6 Wh kg−1 and 1335 W kg−1 respectively. In another study, a supercapacitor with CoFe/WS2 QDs structure exhibited an SC of 551 F/g with a CR of 88.96 % over 6000 cycles [106].

Among the various materials, bismuth-based compounds, such as bismuth tungstate (Bi2WO6) and bismuth oxide (Bi2O3), have demonstrated significant potential for energy storage due to their remarkable properties, which include catalytic activity, ferroelectric characteristics, and stability.

The development of nano-composite materials incorporating these compounds aims to improve the electroconductivity of supercapacitors. The tin oxide (SnO2) nanocrystals have attracted interest for their excellent electrical conductivity, thermal stability, and high capacitance, making them ideal candidates for energy storage applications. The synthesis of SnO2 QDs integrated with Bi2WO6 nanoflowers caused a cost-effective electrode material tailored for energy storage applications. Akkinepally et al. [107] introduced the SSC based on SnO2 QDs/Bi2WO6 nanoflowers electrodes and exhibited an SC of 75.6 F/g with a PD of 14,040 W kg−1. Fig. 12 displays the process of synthesizing SnO2 QDs and the electrochemical assessment of the supercapacitor. A study reported a ternary composite composed of SnO2 QDs, graphene oxide, and PPy exhibited distinctive characteristics, including a substantial SSA and enhanced cyclic stability [108]. The ternary composite achieved an SC of 928.56 F/g with an ED of 25.6 Wh kg−1. In another study, ASC based on SnO2/GO/PPy electrode demonstrated an SC of 1296 F/g, and an ED of 29.6 Wh kg−1 at 5310.26 W kg−1 [109]. Transition metal nitrides have garnered significant interest for their superior electrical conductivity and substantial capacity, with vanadium nitride standing out for its high theoretical capacity and excellent conductivity. However, the practical application of vanadium nitride has been consistently impeded by its solubility issues in alkaline electrolytes. A supercapacitor based on vanadium nitride QDs electrode showed an SC of 278 F/g with an 82.23 % CR after 20,000 cycles [110]. The conducting polymers, particularly PANI and PPy are used in supercapacitors attributed of their electronic conductivity. The ZnS QDs, are used in supercapacitors for their beneficial characteristics, such as high SSA, and thermal stability. Salah et al. [111] introduced PANI/ZnS QDs nanocomposites as novel supercapacitor electrodes for the first time. They found the ASC with PANI/ZnS QDs//graphite electrodes yielded an SC of 358 F/g with an ED of 71.6 Wh kg−1.

A study presented the fabrication of porous carbon nanofibers (CNF) integrated with MOF-derived N-doped porous carbon nanoparticles (NPC) and enhanced with ZnO QDs [112]. The incorporation of NPC nanoparticles significantly enhances the SSA and electrical conductivity of the carbon nanofibers, facilitating electron transfer and reinforcing the mechanical integrity of the structure, while ZnO QDs introduce numerous pseudocapacitive active sites. That study mentioned the ZnO QDs/NPC/CNF electrode achieved an SC of 644.4 F/g. The interaction between the doped metal element and ZnO QDs enhanced the number of active sites, increased the SSA, and created more effective pathways for electron and ion movement [113]. The integration of graphene with hexagonal boron nitride (BN) has garnered significant attention due to their distinctive characteristics. Their analogous crystal structures facilitate the formation of a hybrid material that improves electrical properties by minimizing the band gap and modifying conductivity. This hybrid configuration provides several benefits, including a tunable band-gap, the effect of heteroatom co-doping, and an elevated charge carrier density, positioning it as a suitable candidate for advanced electrode materials. The development of BNQDs covalently integrated with rGO as electrode materials has been described in Ref. [114]. That study demonstrated the A-BNQDs/rGO supercapacitor showed a CR of 94.38 % after 10,000 cycles which was attributed to the strengthened interfacial bonding between BNQDs and rGO. Fig. 13 illustrates the fabrication process of the BNQDs/rGO composite and the electrical characteristics of the supercapacitor. Among various transition metal oxides, mixed metal oxides, particularly NiCo2O4 (NCO-QDs), have garnered significant interest due to their cost-effectiveness, natural abundance, and superior electrochemical performance compared to individual nickel or cobalt oxides. In a study the ASC with NCO5 QDs/rGO1.5 (+)//rGO (−) electrodes achieved an SC of 131 F/g at an ED of 47 Wh kg−1 across a potential window of 1.6 V [115].

Fig. 14 presents a comparative analysis of the Ragone plot, SC, and stability of various supercapacitors utilizing composite electrode materials derived from CQDs, GQDs, and QDs. As depicted in 14(a), the PD of the supercapacitors under comparison is generally below 1500 W kg−1, while the ED remains under 150 Wh kg−1. Fig. 14(b) further illustrates that the SC of supercapacitors is less than 2000 F/g, and Fig. 14(c) reveals that the CR of the supercapacitors averages above 75 %, with cycles fewer than 10,000. This review provides a comprehensive analysis of the utilization of various QDs in constructing supercapacitor electrodes. Extensive investigations have been conducted to improve the operational efficiency of supercapacitors, with QDs being employed in the development of electrodes. GQDs and CQDs possess a less rigid structure but provide benefits such as elevated surface area and rapid electron transport, which are advantageous for energy storage, especially when modified with heteroatoms. CQDs exhibit remarkable electrochemical properties for supercapacitors, attributed to their distinctive quantum size, ease of surface functionalization, and numerous functional groups, highlighting their significant potential in electrode material design for supercapacitors. Direct application of CQDs as electrode materials tends to yield suboptimal electrochemical performance, primarily due to their limited Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area and inadequate electron conductivity [116]. The large-scale production methods and the systematic regulation of CQDs are challenges that hinder their practical implementation in energy storage systems. Investigating the interfacial resistance between CQDs and their respective current collectors is crucial, as this parameter significantly influences the overall performance of the supercapacitor. Achieving well-monodispersed CQDs with high crystallinity is essential for optimal isotropic electrical conductivity.

Therefore, innovative synthesis techniques, purification strategies, and targeted surface functionalization are necessary to refine the architecture of CQDs composites for improved supercapacitor performance. Key attributes of GQDs, including their highly conductive networks, substantial SSA, and diverse surface functional groups, contribute positively to energy-related applications by enhancing pseudocapacitive behavior and reducing charge-transfer resistance. Specific areas requiring additional exploration include the influence of doped heteroatoms on GQD surface properties, the impact of defects and edge states on electrochemical performance, and the need for industrial-scale synthesis of GQDs with high crystallinity and yield. Furthermore, developing stable composite electrode materials is critical, with surface functional groups playing a vital role in achieving long-term cyclic stability, underscoring the necessity for in-depth mechanistic studies on GQD interactions with other materials [117]. Future advancements in QDs-based supercapacitors will depend on sustainable manufacturing processes, as well as a deeper understanding of electron transport mechanisms and the influence of various doping materials and crystal sizes on charge transport efficiency. The future for QDs in supercapacitor technology looks bright, with the continuance of research and innovation. Several aspects need to be considered to make further improvements in the performance and usability of QDs in energy storage systems. The development of innovative and scalable synthesis methods for QDs is hence very essential. Techniques that ensure high crystallinity, homogenous size distribution, and controlled doping with heteroatoms will increase the electrochemical properties of QDs. Their surface properties can be adjusted via targeted functionalization in a way that enhances the interaction between QDs and electrolytes or other materials for better charge transfer efficiency and stability. A deeper understanding of electron transport mechanisms in QDs and their composites is necessary. Other studies must be done to figure out the influence of diverse functional groups on the electrochemical behavior and long-term cyclic stability of QDs-based supercapacitors. Interfacial resistance between QDs and current collectors is the key issue to be addressed for supercapacitor performance optimization. The integration of QDs with other nanomaterials can result in the formation of advanced composite electrodes. Optimization in the architecture of these composites is very important to maximize the surface area, conductivity, and pseudocapacitive behavior of the materials for better energy and power density. The increased demand for flexible and wearable electronic devices will call for the versatility available from QDs-based supercapacitors. With the increasing demand for energy storage solutions, the sustainability of QDs production is of great importance. Therefore, future research must focus on developing eco-friendly materials and processes so that the progress in supercapacitor technology goes hand in hand with environmental goals. Addressing these aspects in the future will unleash the full potential of QDs in the applications of supercapacitors, enabling improvements in efficiency, sustainability, and high performance of energy storage devices.

Every year, scientists conduct significant research into increasing the performance of supercapacitors through the innovation of new materials intended for electrodes and electrolytes. QDs nanomaterials have garnered significant interest among researchers, because of their exceptional electrical characteristics. An investigation of contemporary research indicates that numerous studies have been conducted on innovative techniques for synthesizing QDs from natural raw materials, as well as making composite electrodes with QDs. Within the nanocomposite electrodes, CQDs and GQDs have emerged as the most frequently utilized materials. This review presents a comparative analysis of the performance characteristics exhibited by various QDs-based supercapacitors. The comparative evaluation of the PD and ED of QDs-based supercapacitors typically reveals values that are less than 1500 W kg−1 and 150 Wh kg−1, respectively. Further, the SC of QDs-based supercapacitors illustrates capacitors of less than 2000 F/g with CR of above 75 % and cycles averagely fewer than 10,000. The development of supercapacitors utilizing QDs has significantly contributed to progress in high-efficiency energy storage technologies. The prospects for energy storage seem bright with the advent of QDs-based supercapacitors. Such attributes position them as an optimal and sustainable solution for energy storage.
Ghobad Behzadi Pour: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology. Leila Fekri Aval: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis.
